coredata.dc:title: Implanting N-doped CQDs into rGO aerogels with diversified applications in microwave absorption and wastewater treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Junfeng
Liao, Jun
Wang, Guohui
Du, Rongxiao
Tsidaeva, Natalia
Wang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method, and then implanted into graphene aerogels to obtain N-CQDs/rGO composites. Herein, N-CQDs, as connection sites and channels in rGO, can effectively promote electron jumping and migration and greatly enhance the conduction loss of rGO. As expected, the composites exhibit excellent microwave absorption performance with ultra-wide effective absorption bandwidth of 7.36 GHz and ultra-high minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of −69.42 dB at an ultra-low filler loading of 5 wt%. An ultra-low bulk density of the N-CQDs/rGO composite reach as 4.24 mg/cm3. Actually, the outstanding performance of microwave absorption is attributed to the specific surface morphology and porous structure, enhanced conduction loss and good impedance matching. Meanwhile, the as-synthesized composites display the typical selective adsorption ability for dyes in the wastewater, where the maximum adsorption capacity for methylene blue (MB) dyes is 261 mg/g. Briefly, the study provides a new approach on the comprehensive environmental application of CQDs/graphene composites with both high-performance microwave absorption and high-efficiency removal of organic dyes in the wastewater.

head.ce:title: Implanting N-doped CQDs into rGO aerogels with diversified applications in microwave absorption and wastewater treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method, and then implanted into graphene aerogels to obtain N-CQDs/rGO composites. Herein, N-CQDs, as connection sites and channels in rGO, can effectively promote electron jumping and migration and greatly enhance the conduction loss of rGO. As expected, the composites exhibit excellent microwave absorption performance with ultra-wide effective absorption bandwidth of 7.36 GHz and ultra-high minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of −69.42 dB at an ultra-low filler loading of 5 wt%. An ultra-low bulk density of the N-CQDs/rGO composite reach as 4.24 mg/cm3. Actually, the outstanding performance of microwave absorption is attributed to the specific surface morphology and porous structure, enhanced conduction loss and good impedance matching. Meanwhile, the as-synthesized composites display the typical selective adsorption ability for dyes in the wastewater, where the maximum adsorption capacity for methylene blue (MB) dyes is 261 mg/g. Briefly, the study provides a new approach on the comprehensive environmental application of CQDs/graphene composites with both high-performance microwave absorption and high-efficiency removal of organic dyes in the wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, when we enjoy the convenience and speed brought by the developments in information and communication technology, especially 5G technology, we are also suffering from more and more serious electromagnetic radiation problems caused by the electromagnetic microwave everywhere [1–3]. Therefore, developing high-performance microwave absorption materials become very urgent [4–6]. In the viewpoints of applications, high-quality microwave absorbers should have strong absorption, wide effective absorption band, thin thickness, light weight, physical and chemical stability [7–9]. To the best of our knowledge, microwave absorption materials with ultra-low filler loading and ultra-wide absorption band is still a challenge, which is one of the hot topics in the field of microwave absorption [10,11].

Up to now, a large number of microwave absorbers have been investigated, where the main strategy on enhancing the microwave absorption ability is to improve the magnetic/electric loss and obtain the optimal impedance matching of the as-reported samples. Traditionally, the microwave absorbing materials, such as ceramics, magnetic metals, metal oxides and metal sulfides, are difficult to reach the requirement of lightweight owing to high density [12–14]. In recent years, carbonaceous materials, especially, reduced graphene oxide (rGO), have received particular attention due to their low density, high electrical conductivity and excellent dielectric behavior [15–18]. However, single loss mechanism and impedance mismatch makes them difficult to achieve satisfactory microwave absorption performance [19]. Therefore, we have previously attempted to improve the microwave absorption performance of rGO by combining with other magnetic or dielectric components, such as Co0.8Fe2.2O4/rGO composites [20], rGO/CoS2@MoS2 [21] and rGO/Sm3Fe5O12/CoFe2O4 [22]. Whereas, it should be pointed out that the composites containing rGO and magnetic particles will also suffer from the higher filler volume, which greatly limits their practical applications to a certain extent.

Heteroatoms (N, F, S, P, etc.) doping has thought to be a promising method to improve the absorption performance of graphene-based materials. In fact, the doping of heteroatoms can not only effectively introduce more structural defects in graphene and generate additional polarization relaxation, but also facilitate the induction of disorder in the carbon structure, improve the electrical conductivity of graphene and accelerate the electron transport [23]. Liu et al designed N-doped graphene foam absorbers with high porosity and mesh structure, where superior microwave absorption performance was achieved at an ultra-low filling ratio of 5 wt% [24]. N-rGO aerogels absorbers were obtained with the minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of −56.4 dB and the maximum absorption bandwidth of 6.8 GHz, reported by Shu et al. [25]. We have ever synthesized CoFe2O4/N-doped rGO aerogels with the RLmin of −60.4 dB and effective absorption bandwidth of 6.48 GHz [19]. It is worthy to note that, compared with the graphene/magnetic metal composites, metal-free absorbers based on graphene seem to be easier to achieve lightweight microwave absorbers, including rGO/black phosphorus (BP) [18], rGO/borophene nanosheets (BNS) [26], and rGO/PANI [27], etc. So, one of the main aim in this work is expected to gain a lightweight microwave absorption material without magnetic metals.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in biomedical, sensor, optoelectronic, multicolor imaging, light-emitting diode, photodegradation of organic pollutants and supercapacitor applications due to their high water solubility, biocompatibility, chemical stability, non-toxicity and luminescence [28,29]. Notably, CQDs-based materials also exhibit their application in electromagnetic absorption. For example, Rahal et al prepared a composite microwave absorber of polypyrrole/carbon quantum dots (PPy/CQDs) with low RLmin of −27.8 dB and narrow effective absorption bandwidth of 2.34 GHz [30]. Goel et. al. reported a core–shell CQD@BaTiO3 composite with RLmin of −25.38 dB and narrow effective absorption bandwidth of 2.7 GHz [31]. Apparently, the introduction of CQDs into PPy and BaTiO3 has not brought out the great enhancement of the microwave absorption properties. Now, with the special consideration of the advantage in heteroatom doping, we design a N-doped CQDs and implant it into rGO aerogel to construct a 3D structure, and then, expect to obtain a superior microwave absorption performance of the composite.

Meanwhile, owing to the sheet-like structure, the delocalized π-electron system and the oxygen-containing functional groups on its surface, rGO can usually be used to absorb the contaminants in the wastewater [32]. In our previous work, we prepared the poly(m-phenylenediamine)/rGO/nickel ferrite and CoFe2O4/GO magnetic adsorbents, which can effectively remove dyes and Cr(VI) from aqueous solutions [33]. An ultralight sponge adsorbent based on graphene oxide cross-linked with polyvinyl alcohol was synthesized to remove methylene blue (MB) from the water [34]. A reusable rGO/ZIF-67 adsorbent was synthesized to remove CV and MO from the water [35]. So, the multifunctional application in wastewater treatment and microwave absorption of graphene-based composites should be a very meaningful and valuable attempt [26,33,36].

In this work, we design a high-efficiency multifunctional N-CQDs/rGO aerogels with a 3D hierarchical porous network structure by a one-pot hydrothermal method. The microwave absorption and dyes adsorption capacity of as-synthesized products are investigated, where a RLmin of −69.4 dB with a low filler loading ratio of 5 wt% and the maximum adsorption capacities of 261.8 mg/g for MB are obtained. Moreover, an ultra-wide effective absorption bandwidth reaches up to 7.36 GHz from 10.56 to 17.92 GHz with a thickness of 3.1 mm. Further, the as-prepared N-CQDs/rGO exhibits apparent selective adsorption performance for cation and anion dyes. Then, the main purpose of this study focuses on two aspects: one is to reveal the contribution of heteroatoms doping and CQDs on improving the properties of graphene, and another is to explore the comprehensive multifunctional application in microwave absorption and wastewater treatment of graphene-based materials.
The N-CQDs/rGO composites were prepared by a facile, low-cost and environmental-friendly synthetic route, as shown in Scheme 1 . The specific details are described as follows.

Urea, citric acid, graphite powder (325 mesh), hydrochloric acid, phosphoric acid, concentrated sulfuric acid, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China. All of them were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ethanol and deionized water were used throughout the experiments.

In the experiment, using citric acid as carbon source and urea as nitrogen source, we synthesized fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method. First, 3 g of urea and 3 g of citric acid were added to 20 mL of deionized water under ultrasound, and magnetically stirred for 30 min. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 270 min. Finally, it was naturally cooled to room temperature and let the reaction solution stand for 1 day. Then, the supernatant was obtained by centrifugation at 4500 r/m for 20 min to obtain the N-doped CQDs solution.

As reported previously, graphene oxide (GO) was obtained via a Hummers’ method [33]. Firstly, 0.5 mL of the prepared N-CQDs solution and 6 mL of GO solution (5 mg/mL) were added to 24 mL of deionized water under ultrasound, and then the mixtures were stirred for 6 h at room temperature. Next, the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. Finally, the as-formed hydrogel was soaked into the aqueous alcohol solution for 6 h and then freeze-dried for 48 h. In the hydrothermal process, the residual urea in N-CQDs solution could lead to the reduction of GO. Then, the N-CQDs/rGO aerogels were obtained. Further, using the same method, the other three samples were prepared to discuss the effect of the addition amount of N-CQDs solution on the microwave absorption performance. Here, the addition amount of N-CQDs solution was set as 0, 0.25 and 1 mL, and the corresponding samples were named as rGO, N-CQDs/rGO-5, N-CQDs/rGO-1, respectively.

The crystal structure and crystallinity of the samples were measured by X-ray power diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku, Cu-Kα, λ = 1.5418 Å). Using a Jobin-Yvon spectrometer (LabRAM ARAMIS), Raman spectra were characterized at room temperature. The morphology of the samples was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI TecnaiG220) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi S4700). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250) was applied to characterize the surface chemical state, where twin anode Al Kα X-ray was used as the excitation source. The surface properties were characterized by Brunner-Emmett-Teller surface area analysis (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics instrument Co. USA). The UV absorption spectrum is tested by UV–vis spectrophotometer (PERSEE, TU-1901). In order to obtain the electromagnetic wave absorption properties of the composites, the as-synthesized samples in a certain mass percentage (3 wt%, 5 wt% and 10 wt%) were mixed with the paraffin and pressed as a hollow cylinder (dout = 7.0 mm, din = 3.04 mm). Next, according to the transmission line theory, the electromagnetic parameters of the sample at the frequency range of 2–18 GHz were obtained using the vector network analyzer (VNA, Agilent E8362B) by the coaxial-line method. The specific measure procedure was illustrated detailedly in the supplementary material. In the experiment of testing the electrical conductivity, the N-CQDs/rGO aerogels were pressed as thin sheets at a high pressure of 5 MPa. Subsequently, the conductivities of the composites were measured using the source meter (Keithley 2400) by the four-probe method. The detailed measurement procedures for the conductivity are described in supplementary material.

Fig. 1 a shows the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of N-CQDs, where the main diffraction peaks located at 240 nm and 370 nm are caused by the π-π transition of the C–C bond and the n-π* transition of the -C = O bond, respectively [37,38]. At the same time, a strong bright blue fluorescence can be seen from the aqueous solution of N-CQDs under by an ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm, while it appears dark green color under natural light, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1a [29,31]. Next, XPS spectrum is utilized to analyze the surface composition of N-CQDs. Fig. S1 presents the wide-scan survey spectrum of N-CQDs, where three dominate peaks assigned to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s can be observed clearly. The C 1 s spectrum of the N-CQDs in Fig. 1b can be deconvoluted into four main peaks located at 284.7, 285.1, 286.1 and 288.1 eV, which can be assigned to C–C/C = C, C-N, C-O and C = O, respectively [25,28]. Here, the presence of C-N implies the successful doping of N atoms into N-CQDs. Obviously, the appearance of the C-O and C = O peaks suggest the existence of the oxygen-containing functional groups in the as-synthesized N-CQDs.

The crystal structure and crystallinity of rGO and N-CQDs/rGO are presented by the XRD patterns in Fig. 2 a. As to pure rGO, two diffraction peaks at 24.6° and 43.0° can be indexed to (200) and (100) crystal planes of rGO. Also, the XRD pattern of N-CQDs/rGO is almost the same with that of pure rGO, where the presence of the peaks corresponding to the (200) and (100) crystal planes proves the reduction of GO to rGO during the preparation process [33]. With the comparison to pure rGO, the broader and weaker peaks in N-CQDs/rGO aerogel demonstrate the poorly ordered rGO sheets along the stacking direction owing to the introduction of N-CQDs [28,39].

Normally, Raman spectra are adopted to characterize the graphitization degree of the carbon-based composites. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peaks at 1353 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 correspond to the disordered carbon (D band) and graphitic layers (G band), which are correlated to the sp3 disorder or defects and the sp2 hybridization, respectively. In general, the ratio of ID/IG is often used to represent the graphitization degree, in which a small ratio value suggests a low oxidation level and a high graphitization degree [40,41]. Herein, the ID/IG values of samples rGO and N-CQDs/rGO are 1.05 and 1.19, respectively. Obviously, the increased ratio value implies a low graphitization degree and a higher defect degree, where the increase of defects in rGO may be attributed to the addition of CQDs and N doping modification. Accordingly, the increased defects can give rise to defect polarization in the process of microwave absorption, which contributes to the enhancement of dielectric loss and microwave absorbing ability of the absorbers [41,42]. Furthermore, defects also are beneficial to generate much more active sites on the surface, and then enlarge the dye adsorption ability of the composites.

XPS spectrum is further conducted to investigate the elemental composition and surface chemical status of the as-synthesized N-CQDs/rGO. The the wide-scan survey spectra of rGO and N-CQDs/rGO are shown in Fig. 3 a, where the characteristic peak of N 1 s can only be detected in N-CQDs/rGO. This further proves the successful combination of N-CQDs with rGO. Impressively, the peak intensity ratio (1.66) of C 1 s to O 1 s in N-CQDs/rGO is lower than that (1.73) in rGO, indicating that the introduction of N-CQDs generates more oxygen-containing functional groups. Correspondingly, the high-resolution XPS spectra for C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s are presented in Fig. 3b-d, respectively. In Fig. 3b, the C 1 s spectrum of N-CQDs/rGO shows four characteristic peaks at 284.7, 285.0, 286.1 and 288.2 eV, which can be assigned to C–C/C = C, C-N, C-O and C = O bonds, respectively [19,43]. Herein, the relative peak intensity of the graphitic C–C/C = C bonds is much higher than that of the oxygen-containing functional group, suggesting the removal of some oxygen-containing functional groups and the apparent reduction of GO during the heat treatment process. Also, compared with the peaks in rGO, the existence of the C-N bond in the N-CQDs/rGO aerogels proves the success doping of N atoms into rGO lattice [25,44]. Deconvolution of the O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3c shows three distinct peaks at 513.6, 532.1 and 533.7 eV, corresponding to the residual oxygen-containing functional groups of –COOH, -C = O and -C-OH in graphene [26,27]. In Fig. 3d, the N 1 s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 398.7, 399.9 and 401.2 eV, which can be ascribed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [44,45]. These further verify that the N atoms formed as defects have completely entered into the carbon lattice, which can be taken as the central dipole to adjust the surface electronic state and enhance the conduction loss. Generally speaking, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N can help to the improvement of the dipolar relaxation loss, while graphitic N has a great influence on the conduction loss [25,46]. In fact, pyridinic N mainly comes from the sp2 hybridized N = C bonds at the defects or edges of the carbon plane configuration where one p electron contributes to the π conjugated system, and pyrrolic N originates from the sp3 hybridized N atoms in a five-membered heterocyclic ring where two electrons are offered to the π conjugated system. Whereas, coming from sp2 hybridized N atom, graphitic N adjacent to three sp2 hybridized C atoms can effectively replace the C atoms in graphene lattice. Obviously, the introduction of N results in the destruction of the sp2 carbon domains, the presence of sp3 defect and the increase in disorder sites in the carbon framework, which significantly affects the conductivity and electron distribution on the surface of the composites [28,47].

Then, the lightweight characteristics of the as-synthesized N-CQDs/rGO aerogel is confirmed in Fig. 4 a. It can be seen that the aerogel can be easily place onto the dandelion without leading to any transformation of the dandelion. Meanwhile, with comparison to the pure rGO aerogel, an obvious volume expansion can be found in N-CQDs/rGO aerogel owing to the introduction of N-CQDs. Now, Table 1 gives the the weight, height and cylindrical radius for rGO and N-CQDs/rGO to further reveal the change in the density of the two samples. Here, the obtained N-CQDs/rGO aerogel shows an ultralow bulk density as 4.24 mg/cm3, which is much lower than the previously reported microwave absorbers [25,26,48]. This predicts that the as-synthesized N-CQDs/rGO aerogel could become a superior lightweight microwave absorption material.

Fig. 4b and 4c present the SEM images of the N-CQDs/rGO composite, where abundant pores with the size of 10–50 µm evenly distributed on surface can be clearly observed. Especially, due to the combined effect of N doping and the introduction of CQDs, a specific three-dimensional hierarchical porous structure is shown. Herein, the introduction of CQDs can increase the interlayer spacing of rGO and reduce the agglomeration of rGO nanosheets, leading to a looser structure [28]. Further, the formation of ultra-thin rGO nanosheets can be confirmed by the TEM images of N-CQDs/rGO aerogels in Fig. 4d. At the same time, seen from Fig. 4e and Fig S2, the N-CQDs nanoparticles with the size of 7–10 nm were uniformly anchored on the surface of rGO sheets. Then, Fig. 4f presents the HRTEM image of sample, where the lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.23 nm, corresponding to the basal spacing of (200) lattice plane. Of course, the unique microstructure can bring abundant surface and interfacial characteristics, which is favorable for the improvement of the microwave absorption performance [49].

As mentioned above, the as-prepared N-CQDs/rGO aerogels exhibit a unique 3D laminar porous network structure with ultra-low density. Then, the specific surface areas are analyzed by the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm measurements. Seen from in Fig. S3a, the measured sample displays a traditional IV-type isotherm with a H2-type hysteresis loop, indicating the mesoporous features of the composites [49]. Meanwhile, seen from Fig. S3b, it is clear that the pore size distribution of the aerogels mainly locates in the range of 2–50 nm. Additionally, a large number of macropores can be also observed from the SEM images of Fig. 4b and 4c. So, it can be concluded that the as-synthesized aerogels contain abundant mesoporous and macroporous structures. Also, the BET specific surface area SBET, the pore volume and the average pore diameter of the sample are calculated as 40.06 m2/g, 0.138 cm3/g and 13.78 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the special porous structure is beneficial to obtain a low density of the composite, which is consistent with the above results in Table 1. Similarly, the large SBET and the high porosity can result in generating a rich surface interface to improve both microwave absorption and dye adsorption performance [50].

On the basis of the transmission line theory, the microwave absorption performance of the samples can be evaluated by reflection loss (RL) curve. Then, the RL curve satisfies the following equations.

where Z in is the normalized input impedance of the absorbers, Z 0 is the impedance of the free space, εr and μr is the relative complex permittivity and relative complex permeability, ƒ, d and c are the frequency of microwave, the thickness of the absorbers and the velocity of EMW wave in free space, respectively. When the RL value is equal to −10 dB, it means that 90 % electromagnetic wave will be absorbed by the absorber, and the corresponding frequency range can be named as the effective absorption band (EAB) [15,51].

The RL of the N-CQDs/rGO aerogels with different addition amount of N-CQDs solution are shown in Fig. S4. Herein, at 5 wt% filler loading, owing to the poor dielectric loss, pure rGO exhibit much weaker electromagnetic wave absorption [26]. Furthermore, when the addition amount of N-CQDs is 0.5 mL, sample N-CQDs/rGO exhibits the best microwave absorption performance, indicating that N-doping and the introduction of N-CQDs can greatly improve the microwave absorption properties of rGO aerogels. The detailed analyses on the effect of the addition amount of N-CQDs on the microwave absorption performance of the composites are presented in the supplementary material.

As to high-performance microwave absorbers, low filler loading is one of the important index parameters [25,51]. Therefore, in our experiments, three different filler loading rates of 3, 5 and 10 wt% of N-CQDs/rGO, labelled as S1, S2 and S3, respectively, were systematically analyzed, as shown in Fig. 5 . Seen from Fig. 5a-i, sample S2 possesses the optimal microwave absorption performance with the smallest RLmin and largest EAB at the three filler loading ratios. As to sample S1 with the filler loading ratio of 3 wt%, the RLmin value is just –23.7 dB at 12.96 GHz with the matching thickness of 3.01 mm, and the EAB is 6.56 GHz from 11.44 to 18.00 GHz at 2.87 mm. Then, when the filler loading ratio increases as 5 wt% in the sample/paraffin complex, RLmin reaches up to −69.4 dB at 9.68 GHz with a thickness of 4.09 mm, and the EAB is 7.36 GHz (from 10.56 to 17.92 GHz) at 3.01 mm, completely covering the Ku band. At the same time, it is worth noting that it is possible to achieve selective absorption bands from low frequencies (S- and C-band) to medium frequencies (X-band) and high frequencies (Ku-band), by simply modulating the absorber thickness in 1–5 mm, which facilitates the practical application of microwave absorbing materials. Then, continuing to enlarge the filler loading ratio as 10 wt% will suffer the microwave absorption performance. Here, the RLmin of sample S3 only reaches −26.4 dB at 2.01 mm. Whereas, interestingly, a high EAB keeps as 7.36 GHz (from 10.64 to 18 GHz) at 2.66 mm. The main reason may be that the enhanced dielectric loss capability leads to the poor impedance matching, which prevents more electromagnetic waves from entering the interior of the material and thus leads to high reflection as well as low absorption of electromagnetic waves.

Fig. 5j-l show RL histogram distribution, the highest RLmin and the largest EAB for samples S1, S2 and S3 at some certain thicknesses. Clearly, the sample S2 exhibits the strongest absorption capacity and the relatively larger EAB compared to the other two samples. Further, the microwave absorption performance of the as-synthesized N-CQDs/rGO aerogels is compared with other previously reported graphene-based and CQDs-based microwave absorbers, as shown in Table 2 and in Fig. 6 . It is obvious that the aerogel obtained in our experiment exhibits excellent microwave absorption properties, which can be regarded as a promising candidate for high-performance microwave absorbers.

Further, the quarter-wavelength (λ/4) matching theory is used to explore the relationship of reflection loss with the thickness and frequency. Herein, the matching thickness (tm ) and absorption peak frequency (fm ) can be expressed by the formula [52]:

where λ is the wavelength of incident waves, c is the velocity of light in vacuum, |εr| and |µr| are the modulus of εr and µr , respectively. If t m and f m match the above equation, the reflected electromagnetic waves coming from the air-absorber interface and absorber-metal interface will have a phase difference π, which results in the disappearance of the two electromagnetic waves at the air-absorber interface due to the interference cancellation effect [53]. On the basis of the λ/4 theory, the frequency dependence of RL values with different thicknesses and the matching thickness t m and peak frequency f m of sample S2 are shown in Fig S7, where it can be seen that the microwave absorption behavior of sample S2 obeys the λ/4 theory.

The frequency dependence of the relative complex permittivity (εr = ε′ - jε″) and the relative complex permeability (μr = μ′ - jμ″) are investigated to analyze the microwave absorption essence of the as-prepared absorbers. Also, the corresponding dielectric loss tangent (tanδε = ε″/ε′) and the magnetic loss tangent (tanδμ = μ″/μ′) are calculated to assess the contribution of electric loss and magnetic loss during the process of microwave attenuation for the absorbers [54]. Seen from Fig. 7 a, the values of ε′ decrease from 7.28 to 3.41, 7.66 to 3.34 and 11.09 to 4.22 for the samples S1, S2 and S3, respectively. Similarly, Fig. 7b presents the decrease of ε″ with the increase in the frequency at the range of 1.37–3.27, 1.62–6.18 and 2.68–11.30 for the four samples, respectively. Apparently, at the range of 2–18 GHz, the four samples with ε′ and ε″ values gradually decreased for all four samples. In fact, owing to the electromagnetic energy attenuation, a typical frequency dispersion behavior can be observed. Of course, according to classical frequency dispersion theory, time is enough for the electrons to respond to the alternating electromagnetic field at low frequency, while, at high frequency, electrons cannot keep up with the alternating electromagnetic field in a short period, which causes the decrease in ε′ and ε″ [27,55]. As to the N-CQDs/rGO aerogel, the values of real part ε′ and imaginary part ε″ show an increase trend with increasing loading of samples. This may be caused by the increase in the conductivity of samples with increasing the addition amount of N-CQDs/rGO, owing to the accumulation of the space charges at the interface in the paraffin/sample matrix. Further, the effective medium theory indicates the increase in the polarizable charges with increasing the addition amount of filler loading in the absorbers [25,56]. Therefore, it is reasonable to see that sample S3 with 10 wt% filler loading exhibits the highest ε′ and ε″ vales. On the other hand, in comparison to pure rGO, large number of defects in the rGO lattice, the dipole polarization and the plentiful heterogeneous interfaces between rGO and CQDs exist in N-CQDs/rGO, which leads to the higher ε′ and generates the excellent dielectric loss ability. And then, in terms of Debye theory, the increase in ε″ is mainly determined to the high electrical conductivity and polarization loss in the absorbers [14]. As shown in Figs. S8a and S8b, the μ′ and μ″ approximate to 1 and 0 over the entire frequency region, indicating that the magnetic loss of the composite is negligible. Meanwhile, the values of μ′ and μ″ have small fluctuations in the frequency range of 4–8 GHz, which is caused by small size effects, surface effects and spin wave excitations [12,57]. Fig. 7c and Fig. S8c presents the curves of tanδε ∼ f and tanδμ ∼ f at 2–18 GHz. Herein, it is clear that the value of tanδε is much larger than that of tanδμ over the entire frequency range, indicating the dielectric loss is the dominant factor in the dissipation of microwave energy [27].

Then, on the basis of the Debye relaxation theory, to further reveal the polarization mechanism of the N-CQDs/rGO composites, the ε′ and ε″ can be expressed as.

So, seen from Eq. (5), one Debye relaxation process can be described by one semicircle. Fig. 7d shows the ε′-ε″ curves of the synthesized samples, where multiple semicircles can be observed, indicating the existence of multiple Debye relaxation processes. The Cole-Cole semicircle that can be observed in the N-CQDs/rGO composites implies the presence of more and stronger interfacial polarization and defective dipole polarization. This phenomenon is attributed to the enhanced polarization loss, caused by the generation of more defects, oxygen-containing functional groups and heterojunction interfaces owing to N doping and the introduction of CQDs [11,58].

represents the conductivity. So, a high conductivity will lead to a high ε″ value. Table S1 gives the electrical conductivities of the as-synthesized rGO and N-CQDs/rGO. The corresponding measured σ values are 0.134 and 0.741 s/cm, respectively, which confirms the enhancement of the conductivity for N-CQDs/rGO owing to the implanation of N-CQDs. As mentioned above, N doping will lead to a large amount of graphitic N in the sample, resulting in an elevated conductivity of the composites. At the same time, a large amount of CQDs are dispersed on the rGO nanosheets, forming an effective three-dimensional conductive network structure. And the plentiful folds appearing in the composites lead to the specific mesh-like porous structure. Finally, the three-dimensional conductive network structure with high porosity contributes to the increase in the conduction loss of the N-CQDs/rGO composites [25]. Certainly, seen from the ε′-ε″ curves, the appearance of the long tail is mainly caused by the effect of the conduction loss, which confirms that the conduction loss is also the crucial source of the dielectric loss [59].

In fact, the normalized impedance matching Z and the attenuation constant α are two primary parameters on accessing the the microwave absorption performance of the absorbers. Therefore, according to the transmission line theory, Z and α can be expressed as.

where Zin is the normalized input impedance of the absorbers, Z0 is the impedance of the free space. If Z = 1, the characteristic impedance of a microwave absorber is equal to or close to the characteristic impedance of free space, implying that the incident electromagnetic waves can more easily enter the absorber. Then the incident electromagnetic wave will be further converted into heat energy, and finally an excellent microwave absorption capacity will be obtained. Herein, Fig. 8 a shown the calculated Z values of the samples S1, S2, and S3 at 3.1 mm in the frequency range of 2–18 GHz.

The attenuation constant α reflects the ability and speed on converting the electromagnetic energy to other forms of energies, where the higher α is, the stronger the attenuation ability is. As shown in Fig. 8b, the higher attenuation constant α implies the enhanced attenuation ability of the N-CQDs/rGO composites. Moreover, the variation trend of the attenuation constant α is consistent with that of the complex dielectric constant, which signifies that the attenuation of the incident microwave wave is mainly attributed to the contribution of the dielectric loss. Also, it is obvious that the attenuation constant α increases gradually with increasing the filling ratio owing to the corresponding increase in the dielectric loss [60]. However, from Fig. 8c, sample S2 with filling ratios of 5 wt% exhibits an ultra-wide effective absorption bandwidth of 7.36 GHz and the largest reflection loss of −29.86 dB at the thickness of 3.1 mm. This indicates that the excellent microwave absorption performance of the absorbers is closely related to the comprehensive influence of strong attenuation ability and good impedance matching [61].

Consequently, on account of the above analyses, Scheme 2 presents the possible microwave absorption mechanism of the as-synthesized three-dimensional hierarchical porous network structured N-CQDs/rGO nanocomposites, which can be briefly described as follows. (1) Multiple reflection and scattering. The as-synthesized N-CQDs/rGO aerogels with low density and large specific surface area have established a typical three-dimensional hierarchical porous network structure. This structure will facilitate multiple reflection and scattering of the incident electromagnetic waves inside the absorber and then enhance the attenuation of the entering microwaves into the material [62]. (2) Conduction loss. The introduction of graphitic N and CQDs leads to the formation of the conductive network, which greatly increases the electrical conductivity of the material and accelerates the hopping of electrons accumulated at the interface between CQDs and rGO. It can effectively increase the conduction loss of the composites [63,64]. (3) Polarization loss. The presence of abundant defects, vacancies, functional groups and disordered structures can easily result in the occurrence of the dipole polarization. Meanwhile, the rich interface between CQDs and rGO as well as the local charge accumulation and rearrangement at the interface can greatly give rise to the strong interfacial polarization loss in the action of alternating electric field [19,65,66]. Then, based on the synergistic effect of strong attenuation constants and optimal impedance matching, the prepared N-CQDs/rGO aerogels achieve excellent microwave absorption performance [3,67,68].

The adsorption performance of the as-obtained N-CQDs/rGO composites for cationic dye MB and anionic dye MO was investigated. Fig. 9 a gives the the adsorption curves of MB and MO absorbing onto the composite at different contact time. Owing to the presence of abundant active sites on the surface, a rapid adsorption can be apparently found at the initial 10 min. Notably, a higher equilibrium adsorption capacity for MB dye reaches as 132.22 mg/g, while, as to MO, this is only 6.15 mg/g. As mentioned above, the negatively charged oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aldehyde groups, appearing on the surface of rGO is beneficial to the adsorption of the cationic dye on the basis of the electrostatic interaction. It is known that MB is cationic dye and MO is anionic dye. Therefore, besides the high specific surface area and porous structure, the above difference in adsorption capacity for MB and MO may be mainly caused by the electrostatic interaction and π-π conjugation. Meanwhile, the adsorption mechanism of MB dye was shown in Fig. 9b.

The pseudo-first-order kinetic model and pseudo-second-order kinetic model are used to assess the the adsorption process of MB and MO dyes onto the N-CQDs/rGO composite. The detailed calculations are listed in supplementary material. Fig. S9a plots the curve of log(q e-q t) versus t where the parameters k 1 and q 1e can be obtained from the linear fitting, similarly, k 2 and q 2e are from the linear fitting of t/q t versus t in Fig. S9b. Meanwhile, the initial adsorption rate h (mg g−1 min−1) can be described as h = k 2 q e 2 when t → 0. Table S2 lists the fitting parameters and the correlation coefficient R 2 calculated from the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models. The relatively higher correlation coefficient indicates that the adsorption process for MB and MO dyes meets with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. Additionally, the calculated equilibrium adsorption capacity qcal (132.98 mg g−1) from the pseudo-second-order kinetic model is very close to the experimental value qexp (132.22 mg g−1). Also, a high initial adsorption rate obtained as 301.56 mg g−1 min−1 implies that the as-synthesized composite exhibits the superior rapid adsorption behavior for MB dye. This phenomenon may be caused by the unique morphology as well as the existence of the defects and distortion on the surface.

Further, to reveal the interaction between N-CQDs/rGO composite and the surface of dye molecules, the analyses on the adsorption isotherms for dyes of sample N-CQDs/rGO are carried out. Then, the Langmuir isotherm model and Freundlich isotherm model are used to characterize the corresponding adsorption process taking place on the homogeneous and heterogeneous surface, respectively. As shown in Fig. S10 and Table S3, the detailed discussion is presented in supplementary material, where it is indicated that the adsorption for MB conforms to the Langmuir isothermal model. The maximum adsorption capacity q m calculated from the Langmuir isothermal model reaches as 261.8 mg g−1, which means a promising candidate for cation dye adsorption of the as-synthesized N-CQDs/rGO composite.

In this work, N-doped CQDs were introduced into graphene aerogels through a simple hydrothermal reaction and lyophilization process, and a 3D hierarchical porous network structure with low density and high specific surface area was successfully constructed. Herein, the introduction of N-doped CQDs generates abundant defects, oxygen-containing functional groups and heterogeneous interfaces, which helps to increase conduction loss, dipole polarization and interfacial polarization of the composite. In the meantime, the improved impedance matching of the composite allows more electromagnetic waves to enter the interior of the composite. As expected, the effective absorption bandwidth of the N-CQDs/rGO aerogel composite reaches 7.36 GHz at 3.10 mm with an ultra-low filler loading of 5 wt%. Also, the minimum reflection loss of the composite achieves up to −69.42 dB at 4.09 mm. Besides the superior microwave absorption performance, the composite also exhibits a typical selective adsorption property for cationic dyes. According to the Langmuir model, the maximum adsorption capacities for MB of the as-synthesized N-CQDs/rGO composite is 261.8 mg g−1. In a word, this work provides an innovative design of multifunctional graphene-based composites for comprehensive environmental applications.
